blocks.text: Again I looked up and saw a flying scroll.
And he said to me, “What do you see?” I answered, “I see a flying scroll; its length is twenty cubits, and its width ten cubits.”

1–2 : The scroll is apparently unrolled, rather like a flying carpet, and long, almost 30 ft (9 m). This scroll has a proportion of 2.1, more like a carpet than a document; the Qumran Isaiaha scroll is about 24 ft (7.3 m) long and 11 in (30 cm) wide, a proportion of 24:1.

Then he said to me, “This is the curse that goes out over the face of the whole land; for everyone who steals shall be cut off according to the writing on one side, and everyone who swears falsely shall be cut off according to the writing on the other side.

3 : probably refers to the curses found in God's covenant with Israel (cf. Deut 29.11,13,18), written down on a scroll (cf. 2 Chr 34.24; Dan 9.11). , a phrase used through Zech 1–8 (1.11; 4.10b; 5.6; 6.5). echoes, although does not quote exactly, the Decalogue (Ex 20.7,15,16). Scrolls were rarely written on both sides.

I have sent it out, says the Lord of hosts, and it shall enter the house of the thief, and the house of anyone who swears falsely by my name; and it shall abide in that house and consume it, both timber and stones.”
5.1–4: The sixth vision: a flying scroll.

4 : Destruction of houses is a typical punishment (Hab 3.13b; Dan 2.5; 3.29; Ezra 6.11). Oddly, the perpetrators are not punished. The flying scroll symbolizes the administration of the 's covenant-based justice throughout the world.
Then the angel who talked with me came forward and said to me, “Look up and see what this is that is coming out.”
I said, “What is it?” He said, “This is a basket coming out.” And he said, “This is their iniquity in all the land.”
5–6 : This vision is so bizarre that, unlike the other visions, Zechariah cannot name or describe what he sees; only the angel can do so.

6 : , Heb “ephah,” a unit of measure, ca. two-thirds of a bushel (23 L). Such an amount of grain or flour might have been held in a container made of textile, ceramic, or metal. The referent of is unclear, though it may refer to Judeans, whose iniquity or guilt (see 3.4) is being removed.
Then a leaden cover was lifted, and there was a woman sitting in the basket!c
And he said, “This is Wickedness.” So he thrust her back into the basket,c and pressed the leaden weight down on its mouth.
7–8 : An incongruous image, particularly since an “ephah” could not hold a human. This “ephah” was made of a hard substance due to its lead lid.
Then I looked up and saw two women coming forward. The wind was in their wings; they had wings like the wings of a stork, and they lifted up the basket between earth and sky.
Then I said to the angel who talked with me, “Where are they taking the basket?”a
He said to me, “To the land of Shinar, to build a house for it; and when this is prepared, they will set the basketa down there on its base.”
1.1–8.23: Oracles and visions attributed to Zechariah. This first major division of the book has three parts, each introduced by a date formula (1.1; 1.7; 7.1).
5.5–11: The seventh vision: a woman in a basket.

9–11 : Gender continues to play a prominent role in this vision. with wings, not cherubim, which were male. , an unclean bird (Lev 11.19), and hence, in this simile, well suited to remove iniquity/wickedness , Babylon (Gen 10.10; 11.2; Dan 1.2; Josh 7.21). Isaiah 11.11 understands to be a place for exile and a place from which those exiled will return. Judeans are coming from Babylon; is going to Babylon. , the Babylonians, who will build a shrine for and permanently install , will be polluted by the gesture that purifies Judah.
